label: delphi-informed-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

## Verified-authority annex

Estimate the probability (0–100) that the proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = authorities you actually rely on. biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of. Treat the annex as evidence about the authorities, not as anyone's opinion on the answer.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the text of the governing statute or constitutional provision, read closely.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under art. XIII D, §6(b) — most likely by severing the increment attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" above the documented 1.6x cost differential and/or the rebate-funded share benefiting non-payers — though full, wholesale invalidation is less certain given the partial cost-of-service anchor in the rate study.","rationales":["The text of art. XIII D §6(b)(3) caps a parcel's fee at 'the proportional cost of the service attributable to the parcel,' and the rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — apparently exceeding the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential — creates a direct textual mismatch that the district, bearing the burden under 6(b), will struggle to overcome.","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) and City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (per the verified annex, both actually invalidated tiered rate structures resting on conservation/usage-budget goals rather than proven cost-of-service differences) are squarely on point and cut against a surcharge partly justified by an 'incentive pricing' rationale rather than cost data alone.","Under 6(b)(2), fee revenue 'shall not be used for any purpose other than that for which the fee ... was imposed,' and the district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers is an independent, self-inflicted cross-subsidization vulnerability that reinforces (and is analytically distinct from) the proportionality problem.","Because Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) holds that volumetric/metered water charges are 'property-related fees,' the district's fallback argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is unlikely to succeed, which keeps the surcharge inside 6(b)'s stricter, agency-bears-the-burden framework rather than Prop 26's more forgiving 'reasonable relationship' standard, increasing invalidation risk.","Countervailing: a documented 1.6x cost differential and a debt-service component (30%) plausibly tied to peaking/drought-reserve infrastructure give the district a genuine cost-of-service core (paralleling the upheld cost-based tiered rate in the IID case, 223 Cal.App.4th 892), so a court applying severability principles may well preserve the cost-justified portion of the surcharge and strike only the incentive-pricing excess and rebate-funded share, making full invalidation less likely than partial invalidation."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the \"conservation-incentive pricing\" language in the rate study reflects an actual quantifiable dollar excess over the 1.6x cost-based figure (which would doom that increment) or is merely rhetorical framing around a rate that in fact approximates the cost differential (which could allow the whole surcharge to survive) — the hypothetical does not specify the numeric relationship between the incentive-derived $0.85 and the strictly cost-derived rate, and that gap is the crux of how much of the surcharge, if any, a court would actually strike.","authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(3)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano, 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (Cal. Ct. App.) — cite/holding to be verified","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, 39 Cal.4th 205","Imperial Irrigation District tiered-rate case, 223 Cal.App.4th 892 — cite/holding to be verified","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26) — for the alternative tax-characterization argument","Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2"]}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a court finds at least a substantial part of the Water Resilience Surcharge invalid under art. XIII D, §6(b) — most likely by severing the increment attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" above the documented 1.6x cost differential and/or the rebate-funded share benefiting non-payers — though full, wholesale invalidation is less certain given the partial cost-of-service anchor in the rate study.
rationales: The text of art. XIII D §6(b)(3) caps a parcel's fee at 'the proportional cost of the service attributable to the parcel,' and the rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — apparently exceeding the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential — creates a direct textual mismatch that the district, bearing the burden under 6(b), will struggle to overcome.

Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) and City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (per the verified annex, both actually invalidated tiered rate structures resting on conservation/usage-budget goals rather than proven cost-of-service differences) are squarely on point and cut against a surcharge partly justified by an 'incentive pricing' rationale rather than cost data alone.

Under 6(b)(2), fee revenue 'shall not be used for any purpose other than that for which the fee ... was imposed,' and the district's concession that the 55% rebate allocation mostly benefits customers other than the surcharge-payers is an independent, self-inflicted cross-subsidization vulnerability that reinforces (and is analytically distinct from) the proportionality problem.

Because Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) holds that volumetric/metered water charges are 'property-related fees,' the district's fallback argument that the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all is unlikely to succeed, which keeps the surcharge inside 6(b)'s stricter, agency-bears-the-burden framework rather than Prop 26's more forgiving 'reasonable relationship' standard, increasing invalidation risk.

Countervailing: a documented 1.6x cost differential and a debt-service component (30%) plausibly tied to peaking/drought-reserve infrastructure give the district a genuine cost-of-service core (paralleling the upheld cost-based tiered rate in the IID case, 223 Cal.App.4th 892), so a court applying severability principles may well preserve the cost-justified portion of the surcharge and strike only the incentive-pricing excess and rebate-funded share, making full invalidation less likely than partial invalidation.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the "conservation-incentive pricing" language in the rate study reflects an actual quantifiable dollar excess over the 1.6x cost-based figure (which would doom that increment) or is merely rhetorical framing around a rate that in fact approximates the cost differential (which could allow the whole surcharge to survive) — the hypothetical does not specify the numeric relationship between the incentive-derived $0.85 and the strictly cost-derived rate, and that gap is the crux of how much of the surcharge, if any, a court would actually strike.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano, 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (Cal. Ct. App.) — cite/holding to be verified
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, 39 Cal.4th 205
Imperial Irrigation District tiered-rate case, 223 Cal.App.4th 892 — cite/holding to be verified
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26) — for the alternative tax-characterization argument
